g.data-narration: Welcome to Automata and Complexity Theory - the foundation of computation
Automata theory studies abstract computing machines and the problems they can solve, forming computer science's theoretical backbone.
g.text: The study of abstract computing machines and what problems they can solve
• Foundation of computer science • Compiler design • Pattern matching

g.data-narration: Complexity theory analyzes resources needed to solve problems - time and space - classifying computational difficulty.
g.text: Analyzes resources (time & space) required to solve computational problems
• P vs NP problem • NP-Complete • Algorithm efficiency limits

g.data-narration: A DFA is formally defined as a 5-tuple with states, alphabet, transition function, start state, and accepting states.
DFA has exactly one transition per state-input, while NFA can have multiple or zero, yet both recognize the same languages.
Regular expressions describe regular languages using union, concatenation, and Kleene star operations on basic symbols.
g.text: Three operations to build complex patterns from basic symbols:

g.data-narration: The pumping lemma provides necessary conditions for regularity - if a string in L exceeds length p, it can be divided and pumped.
g.text: If L is regular, then ∃ p ≥ 1 such that ∀s ∈ L with |s| ≥ p:
Used to PROVE non-regularity: L = {0ⁿ1ⁿ | n ≥ 1} not regular

g.data-narration: Context-free grammars generate CFLs with variables, terminals, productions, and a start symbol - essential for programming languages.
g.text: A CFG G = (V, T, P, S) where:

g.data-narration: A pushdown automaton extends finite automata with a stack memory, enabling recognition of context-free languages.
g.text: Finite automaton + stack memory = recognizes CFLs

g.data-narration: A Turing machine has an infinite tape, read-write head, and control unit - the most general model of computation.
The Church-Turing thesis states any effectively computable function can be computed by a Turing machine - computing's fundamental principle.
g.text: "Any effectively computable function can be computed by a Turing machine"
This thesis defines the boundary of computability • Undecidability (Halting Problem) • Recursive vs RE languages

g.data-narration: Complexity classes categorize problems by required resources: P (solvable in poly-time), NP (verifiable in poly-time), and beyond.
g.text: At least as hard as NP

g.data-narration: P vs NP asks: if solutions are quickly verifiable, can they be quickly found? This million-dollar question remains unsolved.
g.text: P vs NP: The Million Dollar Question

g.data-narration: NP-Complete problems include SAT, 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, and Hamiltonian Path - all polynomially equivalent.
g.text: If ANY one is solved in P-time, then P = NP:

g.data-narration: Polynomial reduction transforms problems while preserving solvability - key to proving NP-completeness through known NP-complete problems.
g.text: Transform problem A to problem B in poly-time. If B ∈ P, then A ∈ P

g.data-narration: This DFA accepts binary strings representing numbers divisible by 3, using three states for remainders 0, 1, and 2.
g.text: Example: DFA for Binary Divisible by 3

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: DFA/NFA recognize regular languages, PDA handles context-free, Turing machines model general computation, P vs NP remains open.
